title: Terminal in VS Code
intro: VS Code has a built-in terminal so you never have to leave your editor. It's like having a workbench built right into your garage -- everything you need is within arm's reach. Swipe right if the statement is TRUE, or left if it's FALSE.

teach.title: VS Code Terminal = A Built-In Workbench in Your Garage
teach.body: Imagine a garage where the workbench is built into the wall, right next to your tools. You don't have to walk to a separate room to saw wood or drill holes -- you just look down and there it is. The **VS Code integrated terminal** is that built-in workbench. Your code is on top (the editor), your terminal is on the bottom (the workbench). You see your code and run commands without switching windows. Everything is right there.

teach.title: Integrated Terminal = A Walkie-Talkie Attached to Your Toolkit
teach.body: Picture a construction worker with a **walkie-talkie clipped to their belt**. They're building something with their hands while talking to the team at the same time -- no need to stop working and go find a phone. VS Code's terminal is that walkie-talkie. You're editing code (working with your hands) and running commands (talking to the system) simultaneously, without ever putting your tools down.

teach.title: VS Code Terminal Essentials
teach.body:
Key features of the VS Code integrated terminal:

- **Toggle terminal** -- Ctrl+` (backtick) opens/closes the terminal panel
- **Split terminal** -- Click the split icon to run two terminals side by side (e.g., one for the server, one for commands)
- **Multiple terminals** -- Click the + icon to open new terminal tabs
- **Terminal tabs** -- Switch between terminals using the dropdown or tabs on the right
- **Automatic directory** -- The terminal opens in your project's root folder automatically

teach.title: VS Code Terminal Shortcuts
teach.code:
Ctrl + `           Toggle terminal panel
Ctrl + Shift + `   Create new terminal
Ctrl + Shift + 5   Split terminal
Ctrl + PageUp      Switch to previous terminal
Ctrl + PageDown    Switch to next terminal
teach.annotation: These shortcuts work on Windows and Linux. On Mac, replace Ctrl with Cmd. The backtick key (`) is usually above the Tab key on your keyboard.

teach.body: Professional developers keep their terminal inside VS Code because switching between windows costs time and breaks focus. When you're vibecoding for a client with a deadline, every second counts. One window with code and terminal means you ship faster, debug faster, and earn more per hour.

VS Code's integrated terminal lets you write code and run commands in the same window. Use Ctrl+` to toggle it, split terminals for running multiple tasks, and take advantage of terminal tabs to organize your workflow.

cards.content: You can open the VS Code terminal with Ctrl+` (backtick) to toggle the terminal panel.
cards.explanation: Ctrl+` (or Cmd+` on Mac) is the default keyboard shortcut to toggle the integrated terminal panel in VS Code. It's the fastest way to show or hide the terminal.

cards.content: The VS Code terminal always opens in your home directory, regardless of what project is open.
cards.explanation: The VS Code terminal automatically opens in your project's root directory -- the folder you have open in VS Code. This is one of its biggest advantages over a separate terminal window, because you don't need to cd into your project first.

cards.content: You can split the terminal to run a dev server in one pane and type commands in another.
cards.explanation: Split terminals are incredibly useful. A common setup is running your dev server in one split pane (which stays running) and using the other pane for git commands, installing packages, or running scripts.

cards.content: VS Code's integrated terminal is slower and less capable than a standalone terminal application.
cards.explanation: VS Code's terminal runs the same shell (bash, zsh, PowerShell) as your standalone terminal. It has the same speed and capabilities. The advantage is integration with your editor -- clicking file paths in errors can open the file directly.

cards.content: You can only have one terminal open at a time in VS Code.
cards.explanation: You can have as many terminals as you need in VS Code. Use the + button to create new terminal tabs, or split existing terminals. Many developers run 3-4 terminals: one for the dev server, one for git, one for running tests, and one for general commands.
